items.title: Trichomoniasis: flagellate and alkaline pH
items.contentHtml:
Trichomoniasis

  • Trichomonas vaginalis is a flagellated protozoan; its characteristic mobility is observed in fresh.
  • Vaginal pH is >4.5 (alkaline), which helps in the differential diagnosis with candidiasis (pH <4.5).
  • In fresh examination with saline, motile trophozoites with flagella are seen.
  • Gram stain can show the parasite, but it is not the method of choice; the sensitivity of the fresh is ~60-80%.
  • Trichomoniasis is associated with cervical inflammation (strawberry cervix) and increased risk of infection by human immunodeficiency virus.

items.title: Amsel criteria
items.contentHtml:
Amsel criteria for bacterial vaginosis

  • At least 3 of 4 criteria are required for clinical diagnosis.
  • Criteria 1: homogeneous, grayish-white vaginal discharge lining the vaginal walls.
  • Criteria 2: Vaginal pH >4.5.
  • Criteria 3: positive amine test (fishy odor when adding 10% potassium hydroxide).
  • Criteria 4: presence of clue cells (epithelial cells with adherent coccobacilli) on microscopic examination.
  • Gram stain with the Nugent scale. (score ≥7) is the laboratory standard, but the Amsel criteria are more practical in consultation.

items.foco: Vaginal candidiasis: pseudohyphae and intense itching
items.title: Vaginal candidiasis: pseudohyphae and pruritus
items.contentHtml:
Vaginal candidiasis

  • The most common agent is Candida albicans (~85-90%).
  • The vaginal pH remains <4.5 (normal), unlike bacterial vaginosis and trichomoniasis.
  • The cardinal symptom is intense pruritus and vulvar burning, with lumpy white discharge (type cottage cheese).
  • Microscopic examination with 10% potassium hydroxide shows pseudohyphae and blastoconidia.
  • Predisposing factors: diabetes mellitus, recent use of broad-spectrum antibiotics, corticosteroids, pregnancy.
  • Gram stain also shows Gram-positive yeast and pseudohyphae.

items.foco: Gardnerella vaginalis: clue cells and fishy odor
items.title: Gardnerella vaginalis: clue cells and fishy odor
items.contentHtml:
Gardnerella vaginalis

  • Gardnerella vaginalis is the main microorganism involved in bacterial vaginosis, but it is not the only one (there are anaerobes such as Prevotella and Mobiluncus).
  • The amine test (fishy odor) is produced by the release of amines (putrescine, cadaverine) when adding hydroxide of 10% potassium.
  • The clue cells are vaginal epithelial cells covered with variable Gram coccobacilli that erase the cell borders.
  • The presence of clue cells >20% of the epithelial cells in the microscopic field supports the diagnosis.
  • There is no significant inflammation (scarce leukocytes), which differentiates bacterial vaginosis from trichomoniasis and candidiasis.

items.foco: Vaginosis treatment: oral or topical metronidazole
items.title: Bacterial vaginosis treatment
items.contentHtml:
Treatment of bacterial vaginosis

  • First line: metronidazole 500 mg orally every 12 hours for 7 days, or 0.75% metronidazole gel intravaginally every 24 hours for 5 days.
  • Alternative: clindamycin 2% in vaginal cream every 24 hours for 7 days, or 300 mg orally every 12 hours for 7 days.
  • Treatment of the asymptomatic sexual partner is not routinely recommended (it does not reduce recurrences).
  • In symptomatic pregnant women: oral metronidazole 500 mg every 12 hours for 7 days is safe in all trimesters.
  • Bacterial vaginosis is associated with preterm delivery, so screening and treatment is recommended in pregnant women. high risk.

items.foco: Candida albicans: oral fluconazole single dose
items.title: Candida albicans: oral fluconazole
items.contentHtml:
Vaginal candidiasis due to Candida albicans

  • Treatment of choice for uncomplicated vaginal candidiasis: fluconazole 150 mg oral single dose.
  • Topical alternatives: clotrimazole (vaginal cream or tablet), miconazole, tioconazole (1, 3 or 7-day presentations).
  • The single dose of fluconazole has comparable efficacy to the topical azoles and better adherence.
  • In complicated candidiasis (recurrent, severe, immunocompromised, uncontrolled diabetes mellitus): oral fluconazole 150 mg every 72 hours for 3 doses (days 1, 4 and 7).
  • Do not use fluconazole in the first trimester of pregnancy (category D); topical azoles are preferred.

items.foco: Trichomoniasis: metronidazole and treating sexual partner
items.title: Trichomoniasis: metronidazole and partner
items.contentHtml:
Trichomoniasis: treatment and management of partners

  • Treatment of choice: metronidazole 2 g orally in a single dose or 500 mg orally every 12 hours for 7 days.
  • Alternative: tinidazole 2 g orally in a single dose (better gastrointestinal tolerance).
  • It is mandatory to treat the current sexual partner (although asymptomatic) to avoid reinfection; Sexual abstinence is recommended until treatment is completed.
  • In case of therapeutic failure: rule out reinfection and consider resistance; treat with metronidazole 500 mg orally every 8 hours for 14 days or tinidazole 2 g for 5 days.
  • Trichomoniasis is a notifiable sexually transmitted infection in many countries.

items.foco: Atrophic vaginitis: topical estrogens in postmenopause
items.title: Atrophic vaginitis (vaginal atrophy)
items.contentHtml:
Atrophic vaginitis

  • It occurs in postmenopause due to a decrease in estrogen, with thinning of the vaginal epithelium and an increase in pH (>5.0).
  • Symptoms: vaginal dryness, dyspareunia, mild itching, scant or yellowish discharge, and sometimes postcoital bleeding.
  • The diagnosis is clinical; microscopy shows parabasal and intermediate cells, without leukocytes or pathogens.
  • First-line treatment: topical estrogens (conjugated estrogen cream, estradiol vaginal ring, or estradiol vaginal tablets) in low doses.
  • Systemic estrogens alone are not recommended to treat vaginal atrophy; topicals are safe and effective.

items.foco: Differential diagnosis: flow, pH, and microscopy
items.title: Differential diagnosis of vaginal discharge
items.contentHtml:
Differential diagnosis

  • Vaginal pH: normal <4.5; candidiasis <4.5; bacterial vaginosis and trichomoniasis >4.5.
  • Amine test: positive in bacterial vaginosis and trichomoniasis; negative in candidiasis.
  • Fresh microscopy: in trichomoniasis, motile trophozoites are seen; in bacterial vaginosis, clue cells; in candidiasis, pseudohyphae and spores.
  • Leukocytes: abundant in trichomoniasis and candidiasis; rare in bacterial vaginosis.
  • Flow: homogeneous gray in bacterial vaginosis; lumpy white in candidiasis; foamy greenish-yellow in trichomoniasis.
  • Gram stain complements the diagnosis: variable Gram coccobacilli in bacterial vaginosis, Gram-positive yeasts in candidiasis, and trichomonas can be seen but with lower sensitivity.

items.foco: Bacterial vaginosis: risk of preterm birth
items.title: Bacterial vaginosis and preterm birth
items.contentHtml:
Bacterial vaginosis in pregnancy

  • Bacterial vaginosis is associated with an increased risk of preterm delivery, premature rupture of membranes and chorioamnionitis.
  • The proposed mechanism is the rise of anaerobic bacteria that produce phospholipases and proinflammatory cytokines, triggering uterine contractions.
  • Screening with culture or screening criteria is recommended. Amsel in pregnant women with a history of previous preterm birth.
  • Treatment with oral metronidazole or clindamycin reduces the risk of preterm birth in women with bacterial vaginosis and a history of preterm birth.
  • In asymptomatic pregnant women without risk, universal screening is not routinely recommended.

items.foco: Recurrent candidiasis: culture and prolonged treatment
items.title: Recurrent candidiasis (≥4 episodes/year)
items.contentHtml:
Recurrent vaginal candidiasis

  • It is defined as ≥4 symptomatic episodes in 12 months.
  • The diagnosis must be confirmed with culture (to identify the species and rule out non-albicans species such as Candida glabrata that are resistant to fluconazole).
  • Induction treatment: fluconazole 150 mg orally every 72 hours for 3 doses. (days 1, 4 and 7).
  • Maintenance treatment: fluconazole 150 mg orally once a week for 6 months.
  • Maintenance alternatives: clotrimazole 500 mg vaginally once a week, or itraconazole 100 mg orally every 24 hours for 7 days a month.
  • Predisposing factors must be corrected: strict glycemic control in diabetes mellitus, avoid unnecessary antibiotics.

items.foco: Coinfection: HPV and common bacterial vaginosis
items.title: HPV and bacterial vaginosis coinfection
items.contentHtml:
Coinfection: human papillomavirus and bacterial vaginosis

  • Bacterial vaginosis is common in women with high-risk human papillomavirus (HPV) infection.
  • It is postulated that alteration of the vaginal microbiome (decrease in lactobacilli and increase in anaerobes) favors the persistence of HPV and the progression to intraepithelial lesions
  • The presence of bacterial vaginosis is associated with a higher HPV viral load and a lower viral clearance rate.
  • There is insufficient evidence to recommend treatment of bacterial vaginosis as an adjuvant therapy to eliminate HPV, but it is suggested to treat symptomatic bacterial vaginosis.
  • In women with abnormal cervical cytology, it is reasonable to evaluate and treat bacterial vaginosis if present.
